summary: Gruber critiques the theory that Facebook Home failed because its designers were iPhone users who didn't understand Android. He argues the real problem is simpler: Facebook Home is a bad idea, period. The product replaces core phone functionality with a Facebook-centric experience nobody asked for. Gruber notes that even the iPhone-user explanation doesn't hold up, since iPhones have docks and folders too. The deeper dogfooding failure isn't about platform familiarity—it's that Facebook's own people don't want to use what they built. If your own team prefers competing products, that's a fundamental product problem.
key_points: The theory that iPhone-carrying Facebook engineers caused Home's failure is an unsupported conclusion—the missing features like docks and folders exist on iPhone too
Facebook Home is a well-designed implementation of an idea no one wants: replacing your home screen with a single service
Facebook is an app, not a platform, and a good home screen should accommodate any app or service
The real dogfooding failure is that Facebook's own designers and engineers prefer iPhones over the product they built
Products that their own creators don't want to use are doomed to fail
money_quotes: Facebook Home is just a bad idea.
It's a well-designed implementation of an idea no one wants.
Facebook Home isn't an iPhone idea. It's just a bad idea.
Facebook is an app, not a platform.
A good home screen interface is one that accommodates any app or service, not just one.
It's always a sign of trouble when you've built something you don't want to use yourself.
Why does everyone I know who works at Apple carry an iPhone? Every single one? Not because they have to. It's because they want to.
Turn Facebook Home into an interface that Facebook designers and engineers want to use, not merely feel obligated to use, and then they'll have something.
key_insight: The truest test of a product isn't market research or user testing—it's whether the people who built it actually want to use it themselves.
